agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Subscription billing platform managing lifecycle states (activation, renewal, cancellation, trials) with payment validation, retry logic, and tier-specific grace periods.","bounded_context":"Subscription Billing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Subscription","definition":"A recurring billing agreement between a customer and the platform, with a defined billing cycle and tier."},{"term":"Payment Method","definition":"A stored instrument (card, bank account) used to collect subscription fees; has a validity/status (active, failed)."},{"term":"Billing Cycle","definition":"The recurring period (e.g., monthly, annual) that defines when payment is due."},{"term":"Renewal","definition":"The automated attempt to collect payment and extend the subscription for another billing cycle."},{"term":"Grace Period","definition":"A time window after failed payment during which the subscription remains active before cancellation."},{"term":"Free Trial","definition":"A zero-cost subscription period offered to new customers, limited to one active trial per email address."},{"term":"Premium Tier","definition":"A subscription level that grants enhanced benefits, including a 14-day grace period on failed renewals."},{"term":"Failed Payment Method","definition":"A payment instrument that has been declined or is otherwise unable to process charges."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A subscription cannot be activated with a failed payment method on file.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. Activating a subscription against a known-bad payment method would create a subscription that can never collect revenue — the system would be in a corrupt state from inception. No business policy change would ever allow deliberately onboarding a customer with a broken payment method; doing so would mean the subscription's financial obligation has no backing instrument, violating data consistency between the subscription state and its payment capability.","testable_assertion":"Given a payment method with status 'failed', any attempt to activate a subscription using that payment method MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Subscriptions auto-renew 7 days before the billing cycle ends to allow retry time.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 7-day lead time is a business policy decision designed to provide a retry window. A VP or product team could reasonably change this to 5 days, 10 days, or any other window based on payment processor retry policies, customer feedback, or operational data. The system is not corrupt if this number changes — it is a tunable threshold.","testable_assertion":"Given an active subscription, renewal processing MUST be triggered exactly 7 days before the billing cycle end date."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"After 3 failed renewal attempts, the subscription is automatically cancelled.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 3-strike threshold is a business policy balancing revenue recovery against bad-debt risk. A product team could change this to 2 or 5 attempts based on churn analysis or payment processor recommendations. The system is not in a corrupt state if this number changes — it is a configurable policy parameter. Note: this rule applies to non-Premium subscribers (see BR-5).","testable_assertion":"Given a non-Premium subscription with 3 consecutive failed renewal attempts, the system MUST automatically transition the subscription to 'cancelled' status."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A cancelled subscription cannot be reactivated — customers must create a new subscription.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint enforcing the irreversibility of the 'cancelled' terminal state. Allowing reactivation of a cancelled subscription would break the subscription lifecycle state machine, creating ambiguity around billing history, proration, and audit trails. The cancelled state is a terminal node — permitting transitions out of it would corrupt the state machine's integrity. If the business wanted to allow re-subscription, the correct domain model is to create a new subscription entity, preserving the historical record.","testable_assertion":"Given a subscription in 'cancelled' status, any attempt to transition it to 'active' MUST be rejected. The only path forward is creating a new subscription entity."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium tier subscribers get a 14-day grace period before cancellation instead of the 3-strike rule.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 14-day grace period and the decision to use a time-based window instead of attempt-counting for Premium tier are business policy choices reflecting the higher value of Premium customers. A VP could change this to 21 days, 10 days, or even revert Premium to a strike-based model. The system is not corrupt if these parameters change — it is a tier-specific policy preference.","testable_assertion":"Given a Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal, the system MUST NOT cancel the subscription until 14 days have elapsed since the first failed renewal attempt, regardless of the number of retry attempts within that window."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Free trial subscriptions cannot have more than one active trial per email address at any time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and uniqueness constraint. Allowing multiple concurrent active trials for the same email would represent a corrupt state — the system would be giving away multiple free periods to the same identity, which is not a policy tuning question but a fundamental integrity violation. It is analogous to a uniqueness constraint in a database: the same email cannot occupy two 'active trial' slots simultaneously. While the business could change whether trials exist at all, the one-active-trial-per-email rule while trials are offered is a hard constraint on data integrity.","testable_assertion":"Given an email address with an existing active free trial subscription, any attempt to create a second free trial subscription for that same email MUST be rejected."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer attempts to activate a subscription","when":"The payment method on file has a 'failed' status","then":"The system rejects the activation and returns an error indicating the payment method must be updated"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An active subscription exists with a billing cycle ending in 7 days","when":"The renewal scheduler runs","then":"A renewal payment attempt is initiated for that subscription"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A non-Premium subscription has accumulated 3 consecutive failed renewal attempts","when":"The third failure is recorded","then":"The subscription is automatically transitioned to 'cancelled' status"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A subscription is in 'cancelled' status","when":"Any API call or user action attempts to reactivate it","then":"The system rejects the request and instructs the customer to create a new subscription"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Premium tier subscription has a failed renewal","when":"Fewer than 14 days have elapsed since the first failure in this renewal cycle","then":"The subscription remains active and renewal retries continue"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A Premium tier subscription has a failed renewal","when":"14 days have elapsed since the first failure without a successful payment","then":"The subscription is automatically cancelled"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An email address already has an active free trial subscription","when":"A request is made to create another free trial for the same email","then":"The system rejects the request and returns an error indicating only one active trial per email is allowed"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Payment method fails between validation and activation (race condition)","expected_behavior":"The activation must re-validate payment method status at the moment of state transition, not rely on a prior check. Use optimistic locking or a transactional guard."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber downgrades to standard tier during the 14-day grace period","expected_behavior":"The system must decide which cancellation policy applies. Recommendation: the policy active at the time the first failure occurred should govern the entire grace/retry cycle."},{"scenario":"Customer updates payment method to a valid one after 2 failed attempts (non-Premium)","expected_behavior":"The failed attempt counter should reset upon a successful payment, preventing cancellation."},{"scenario":"Free trial subscription is cancelled and the same email immediately requests a new trial","expected_behavior":"Since the original trial is no longer 'active', the one-active-trial constraint is satisfied. However, the business should decide whether re-trialing is permitted at all (abuse prevention is a separate concern from the uniqueness invariant)."},{"scenario":"Renewal triggered at day -7 succeeds, but the payment is later reversed/charged back","expected_behavior":"The system must handle post-success reversals — a successful renewal that is later reversed should re-enter the retry flow, not silently leave the subscription active with no collected payment."},{"scenario":"Clock/timezone edge: billing cycle end date falls on a DST boundary","expected_behavior":"The 7-day and 14-day windows must use a consistent timezone or UTC to avoid off-by-one errors in trigger timing."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Incorrect invariant enforcement directly impacts revenue: allowing activations on failed payment methods creates non-paying subscribers; premature cancellation of Premium subscribers increases high-value churn; trial abuse erodes acquisition unit economics.","operational":"The retry window (7 days) and strike count (3) determine payment operations load and support ticket volume. Grace period logic for Premium tier adds complexity to the billing scheduler that must be carefully tested.","user":"Customers expect predictable billing behavior. Premature cancellation frustrates loyal Premium subscribers. Trial uniqueness enforcement must have clear error messaging to avoid confusion for legitimate customers.","risk":"Primary risks: (1) race conditions in payment validation during activation, (2) tier-change during grace period creating policy ambiguity, (3) trial abuse via email aliasing (plus-addressing) which is outside the stated rules but a real-world concern.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers (all tiers)","impact":"Directly affected by activation gates, cancellation policies, and trial limits. Premium customers benefit from extended grace period."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"Cancellation thresholds and retry windows directly determine bad-debt write-offs and revenue recovery rates."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will handle escalations from cancelled subscribers who want reactivation — must be trained on the 'new subscription' policy."},{"group":"Product/Growth team","impact":"Trial uniqueness constraint affects acquisition funnel conversion; too strict may deter signups, too loose enables abuse."},{"group":"Payment Operations","impact":"Retry cadence and grace period logic determine integration load with payment processors."}]},"confidence":{"level":82,"reasoning":"The spec is well-defined with clear rules and minimal ambiguity. The six rules are concrete and testable. Confidence is not higher because: (1) the interaction between Premium grace period and the 3-strike rule needs clarification — does Premium skip strikes entirely or just extend time? (2) The spec is silent on what happens when a customer updates their payment method mid-retry. (3) Trial uniqueness is per-email but the spec doesn't address email aliasing or account-level constraints.","high_confidence_areas":["BR-1: activation gate on failed payment is clearly an invariant","BR-4: cancelled-is-terminal is clearly an invariant","BR-6: one-trial-per-email is clearly a uniqueness invariant","BR-2 and BR-3: clearly tunable policy parameters"],"low_confidence_areas":["Interaction between Premium grace period (BR-5) and standard retry logic (BR-3) — does Premium replace or augment the strike counter?","Behavior when payment method is updated mid-retry cycle","Trial re-creation after cancellation — uniqueness vs abuse prevention boundary"],"assumptions":["The 14-day Premium grace period completely replaces the 3-strike rule (not augments it)","Failed attempt counter resets on successful payment","Email matching for trial uniqueness is exact string match, not normalized"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"BR-5 says Premium gets a 14-day grace period 'instead of the 3-strike rule.' It is unclear whether Premium subscribers have unlimited retry attempts within 14 days, or if there is still some maximum attempt cap. Also unclear: what happens if a Premium subscriber downgrades during the grace period?","affected_stakeholders":["Finance/Revenue team","Customer Support","Premium subscribers"],"options":["A: Premium has unlimited retries within 14 days, no strike counter applies","B: Premium has 14-day window but still caps at some number of retries (e.g., daily retries = ~14 attempts)","C: The more generous of the two policies applies at any given moment"],"recommendation":"Option A — the spec explicitly says 'instead of' the 3-strike rule, implying the time-based window fully replaces attempt counting for Premium."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The spec does not define what happens when a customer updates their payment method to a valid one during an active retry/grace period. Should the failed attempt counter reset? Should the grace period timer reset?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Customer Support","Payment Operations"],"options":["A: Reset counter/timer on successful payment with new method","B: Counter/timer persists — new method is tried on next scheduled attempt","C: Immediate retry with new method, reset on success"],"recommendation":"Option C — best customer experience and aligns with industry standard dunning practices."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
